title: Engineering Services

labor_share.source: BLS Industry Productivity via FRED — Labor Compensation (IPUMN541330L020000000) and Sectoral Output (IPUMN541330T300000000) for NAICS 541330

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Engineer-of-record stamp, liability, client trust are non-automatable; analysis and report-writing hours are heavily AI-exposed. 25% fraction conservative for report drafting, literature review, calculations review.
Calculations, modeling and report drafting automate substantially; field verification and judgment calls persist. 50% fraction covers document production and routine analysis.
Lab/materials testing and data collection partly physical; data reduction and report prep automate. 35% fraction.
Production drawings are the most automatable cost line; generative AI tools already produce draft sets. 25% retained for quality review, jurisdictional compliance, and revision cycles.
Client/agency coordination persists; schedule, budget and submittal paperwork automates. 30% fraction.
Physical site presence (construction inspection, surveying) — AI-resistant, drone-augmented only at margins. 5% for data processing back in office.
Proposal production for QBS pursuits is a large, automatable overhead. 65% for drafting, formatting, boilerplate. 35% retained for strategic pursuits and relationship-based writing.

ai_replaceable_share.source: BLS OEWS May 2023 NAICS 541330 occupation table for role mix; prompt guidance for automatable fractions

pi_dist.evidence: Public-sector clients (DOTs, municipalities; ~50% of revenue) select via QBS on on-call/MSA contracts held by the firm, but re-competed every 3-5 years; individual principals matter for retention. Private/developer clients (~30%) hold closer relationships at firm level. Industrial (~15%) are more personal. FAR cost-plus audited overhead means AI efficiency gains on public work reduce billable cost and are passed to clients rather than retained as margin — a direct pi_dist depressant. Engineer-of-record is institutional to the firm, not entirely personal. Net score: 0.55 reflecting partial firm-level control diluted by QBS re-competition and cost-plus billing for public share.
sources: FAQs: Overhead Rate Audits & FAR Part 31 for A&E Firms — Cherry Bekaert (https://www.cbh.com/insights/articles/faqs-overhead-rate-audits-far-part-31-for-ae-firms/)

pi_moat.evidence: PE licensure (engineer-of-record stamp) creates legal barrier — every state requires a PE in responsible charge, blocking AI-only bypass for any stamped deliverable. Firm registration requirements add layer. However: (1) FAR/AASHTO overhead audits reduce AI-driven cost advantages for public work (clawback channel); (2) QBS re-competition every 3-5 years limits lock-in; (3) developer/private clients can adopt plan-stamping (insource with AI + single stamping PE) though responsible-charge rules constrain spread; (4) proprietary project data and records create moderate switching cost. Net: licensure provides genuine but imperfect moat; pricing structure deflates margin capture. Score 0.55.
sources: FAR Part 31 overhead audit structure — Cherry Bekaert, GMA CPA (https://www.gma-cpa.com/blog/far-overhead-rates-and-audits-for-a/e-firms-faqs)

current_adoption_pct.source: Deltek Clarity 46th Annual A&E Industry Study (2025, published 2025); 2024 press release: '53% of firms already use AI and machine learning; two-thirds have embraced generative AI'. Narrowed to material use in automatable production functions (not just experimentation) from 53% headline to ~32% using judgment that proposal-level AI is not the same as production-workflow AI.

historical_analogs.value: CAD adoption from manual drafting: ~10 years to reach 50% (early 1980s to early 1990s). BIM adoption: ~8-10 years from early adopters (~2004) to ~50% (~2013-2015). Both analogues involve similar professional inertia, licensure-related caution, and capital investment.
historical_analogs.source: Industry knowledge; no single primary source for analog comparison. Secondary: AIA and ENR historical adoption curves discussed in trade literature.

t50_years.derivation: Current material adoption ~32% (mid-2026). Historical analogues (CAD, BIM) took 8-10 years total to 50%. AI adoption has been tripling YoY (Deltek). Starting from near-zero ~2022, 4-year S-curve would put 50% around 2026-2028. Given current 32% and tripling YoY trend slowing to 1.5-2x per year as diffusion broadens, estimate 3 more years (2029) to reach 50% material adoption in automatable functions. t50 = 3 years.

n_total.source: naicslist.com / Census-derived; 38,516 companies active in NAICS 541330 with estimated employment of 1,098,479

n_band.derivation_chain: Step 1: Zweig Group 2024 median firm: 38 FTE, NSR $6.3M, EBITDA $965K (~15.3% EBITDA margin). At 15% EBITDA margin, $1M EBITDA threshold corresponds to NSR ~$6.5M, $10M EBITDA to NSR ~$65M. Step 2: SUSB employment size distribution — most AE firms are 1-9 employees (micro-practices, sole proprietors below $1M EBITDA threshold). Firms with 10-49 employees likely to have $1M-$5M EBITDA; firms 50-249 employees likely $5M-$15M EBITDA. Step 3: Of 38,516 firms, approximately 50% are below 10 employees and thus below $1M EBITDA (consistent with Zweig median being the ~50th percentile at $965K). Step 4: ~30% of firms fall in 10-249 employee range = ~11,555 firms. Of these, roughly 60-65% have EBITDA below $10M (upper end served by firms 250+ employees). n_band estimate: 11,555 × 0.6 = ~6,933, rounded to 7,000. Quality: ESTIMATE — SUSB 6-digit breakdown not directly fetched.

owners_60plus_pct.source: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report notes 'nearly 80 percent of firm owners looking to transition in the next decade' — implies large share aged 55+. ACEC Research Institute OTMS survey and workforce analysis confirms aging ownership base. 45% aged 60+ is a conservative estimate consistent with documented succession shortage.

active_consolidators.source: Morrissey Goodale 2024 M&A data and Auxo Capital 2025 data: 25+ PE-backed platforms named (Salas O'Brien, Verdantas, WSB, IMEG, Ardurra, Consor, WGI/First Reserve, CPL/GHK Capital, RTM/MML Capital, Olsson/MSCP, Bowman/Nasdaq, NV5/Nasdaq, Pennoni, Mainstay, and 15+ others). PE-backed share of AE M&A exceeded 50% of all deals in 2024-2025. Count conservatively at 30 active add-on platforms in last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: Zweig Group 2024 Valuation Report of AEC Firms: median value/EBITDA = 4.23 for all-size survey. Auxo Capital 2026 guide: smaller founder-led engineering firms 3.5x-5.5x adjusted EBITDA. Normalized buy multiple for single-location/$1-5M EBITDA firm: 4.5x (above Zweig median to account for EBITDA normalization per rule 3 — owner comp added back to normalize).

exit_mult.source: Auxo Capital Advisors 2026 guide: platform-quality engineering $10M+ EBITDA at 8.5-12x EBITDA. Search results cite PE paying avg 10.6x for construction/engineering 2018-2025; WSP/TRC deal at 15.9x (strategic outlier). Conservative platform exit at $10M+ EBITDA: 9.0x as midpoint of 8.5-10x for established multi-state platform (below strategic premium).

V.arithmetic: V_raw = labor_share × ai_replaceable_share = 0.470 × 0.378 = 0.1777. V = round(10 × min(1, 0.1777/0.25)) = round(10 × 0.7108) = round(7.108) = 7

C.arithmetic: C = round(10 × pi_dist × pi_moat) = round(10 × 0.55 × 0.55) = round(10 × 0.3025) = round(3.025) = 3

A.arithmetic: A = min(10, round(10 / t50_years)) = min(10, round(10/3.0)) = min(10, round(3.333)) = min(10, 3) = 3

B.arithmetic: TD: n_band = 7000, which is >5000 → TD = 1.0. OW: owners_60plus_pct = 0.45 (>35%) → 0.8; +0.1 for documented succession shortage (Zweig confirms) → OW = 0.9. PD = max(0, (exit_mult - buy_mult)/exit_mult) = max(0, (9.0-4.5)/9.0) = max(0, 0.5) = 0.5. CFD: active_consolidators = 30, which is in 11-30 range → CFD = 0.6. WAIT — 30 is at boundary. The formula says: ≤2→0.1; 3-10→0.3; 11-30→0.6; >30→0.9. active_consolidators = 30 falls in 11-30 → CFD = 0.6. B = round(10 × (TD × OW × PD)^(1/3) / (1 + 0.3 × CFD)) = round(10 × (1.0 × 0.9 × 0.5)^(1/3) / (1 + 0.3 × 0.6)) = round(10 × (0.45)^(1/3) / (1 + 0.18)) = round(10 × 0.7663 / 1.18) = round(10 × 0.6494) = round(6.494) = 6

M.arithmetic: M = min(10, max(0, round((exit_mult - buy_mult) / buy_mult × 4))) = min(10, max(0, round((9.0-4.5)/4.5 × 4))) = min(10, max(0, round(1.0 × 4))) = min(10, max(0, 4)) = 4

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw = 0.1777 → theoretical EBITDA uplift of 17.77pp (gross). However, ~50% of AE revenue flows through FAR/AASHTO cost-plus audited contracts where AI-driven labor hour reductions reduce overhead recovery and billing directly — client captures efficiency gain, not the firm. Effective retained uplift for the operator ≈ 17.77 × 0.50 (private/fixed-fee share) = ~8.9pp. With A=3 (moderate-slow adoption), this is consistent: significant but partially captured value being unlocked gradually. No contradiction requiring revision.
cross_checks.terminal_value: Near-term (1-5 years): PE stamp, EOR liability, and infrastructure-funding demand (IIJA/BIL) make engineering services paid demand robust. AI cuts production hours but firms still need licensed engineers to stamp, and regulatory frameworks require PE in responsible charge — demand is not being replaced, hours are being reduced. Medium-term (5-10 years): the critical risk is whether AI-collapsed production hours convert this into a smaller-headcount stamp-and-liability business. For public cost-plus clients, FAR overhead audits translate AI-driven overhead reductions directly into lower billing multipliers — the productivity gain accrues to taxpayers/clients, not to owners. For fixed-fee and private clients, the firm captures margin expansion. The key distinction is: AI is cutting operator costs (good for firm on fixed-fee), but on the dominant public-sector side, the cost-plus pricing model converts this cost reduction into client savings. This makes the terminal value ambiguous and dependent on client-mix evolution.
cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V score derived from FRED BLS labor compensation/output ratio (primary source, no deal activity) and OEWS occupation breakdown (primary source). A score derived from Deltek Clarity AI adoption survey of A&E firms (primary survey, no M&A reference). Neither V nor A was informed by PE deal count, consolidation waves, or acquisition multiples.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Material deflation channel confirmed. Engineering services are priced primarily as: (1) hourly/cost-plus with FAR overhead multiplier for federal and state DOT work (~40-55% of sector revenue); (2) QBS negotiated lump sum/hourly for municipal work (~10-15%); (3) fixed-fee/lump-sum for private developer and industrial clients (~30-40%). Under cost-plus/FAR billing: AI reduces direct labor hours → reduces overhead base → reduces total billing → efficiency gain flows to client, not firm. This is a structural mechanism confirmed by FAR Part 31 overhead audit literature. Under fixed-fee billing: AI reduces cost while revenue is fixed → firm captures full margin. The ~50% public-sector cost-plus exposure is the primary deflation channel. Reflected in pi_dist = 0.55 and pi_moat = 0.55 — these discount AI's value to firm operators specifically because of the pricing-model clawback on the public side.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from min(10,round(10/2))=5 to min(10,round(10/5))=2; central estimate A=3. S changes from 504 to 840 (t50=2) or 336 (t50=5). Largest single-input uncertainty.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 3,000-10,000 firms with $1-10M EBITDA; TD stays at 1.0 above 5,000 so lower bound matters: if n_band < 5,000 then TD=0.8 and B drops to round(10 × 0.6494 × (0.8/1.0)) ≈ 5
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: B could drop from 6 to 5 if n_band < 5,000. S would change from 504 to 420.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: If private-sector share higher or QBS stickier: pi_dist=0.65, pi_moat=0.60 → C=round(10×0.39)=4. If cost-plus dominates more: pi_dist=0.45, pi_moat=0.50 → C=round(10×0.225)=2
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: C ranges 2-4; S changes from 504 to 336 (C=2) or 672 (C=4).

reviewer_note: S_product = 7 × 3 × 3 × 6 × 4 = 504. The dominant tension in this industry is the FAR/cost-plus pricing deflation channel: for ~50% of revenue on public cost-plus contracts, AI efficiency gains flow to clients through overhead audit clawbacks rather than to firm operators. This structurally suppresses C (scored 3) despite high V (scored 7). The industry has robust M&A infrastructure (B=6, M=4) and high AI applicability (V=7) but moderate adoption speed (A=3) and constrained margin capture. Active consolidators counted at 30 (boundary of 11-30 vs >30 band) — if re-counted at 31+ (conservative given 25+ explicitly named plus others), CFD = 0.9 and B = round(10 × 0.7663 / 1.27) = round(6.034) = 6 unchanged because the B formula denominator increases. EBITDA normalization note: Zweig Group median EBITDA of $965K already reflects a pre-bonus figure; for strict rule 3 normalization, owner draws at market rate should be added back to compute true EBITDA, which would reduce the multiple applied at buy_mult. The 4.5x buy multiple is already conservative vs Zweig's 4.23x to account for this. The plan-stamping risk (clients insource with AI + single stamping PE) is real but currently constrained by responsible-charge rules and client risk tolerance for E&O exposure.
